text: This is Dr. Anusha Shah presenting a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal ventral hernia repair. The patient is a fifty-year-old male with a symptomatic ventral hernia with past medical history of high blood pressure and a BMI of forty. The patient had no history of previous abdominal surgeries.

The surgeon reduces the contents of the umbilical hernia using blunt and sharp dissection with ProGrasp forceps and scissors. After reduction, a 2x3 cm umbilical defect is visible. The peritoneum ipsilateral to the trocar sites is positioned at least 5 cm away from the proximal edge of the defect, providing space for adequate mesh overlap.

The surgeon uses scissors for blunt dissection to separate the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath, revealing the underlying fat of the rectus muscle. The dissection progresses medially and laterally, aided by adequate retraction to identify stretch fibers and maintain correct planes. The hernia sac is identified and dissected both cephalad and caudad to delineate its location before reduction.

The surgeon uses adequate retraction to clearly visualize and dissect the peritoneum off the posterior sheath. The arcuate line is visible on the screen, serving as a landmark for identifying the inferior epigastric vessels. An arrow marks the location of the hernia sac, and dissection proceeds cephalad to the sac.

The surgeon partially encircles the hernia sac and begins its reduction. The fibers of the transversalis fascia are incised to achieve true preperitoneal reduction. Incarcerated preperitoneal fat is visually distinguished from the transversalis fascia and reduced along with the peritoneal sac.
The surgeon uses adequate retraction to reveal the limits of the peritoneal sac. After reducing the sac, preperitoneal dissection is performed on the contralateral side, separating the tissue layers.

The surgeon employs a retract, buzz, and push technique to separate the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath, demonstrating the difference between adequate and inadequate retraction. Articulated scissors are used to bluntly dissect the peritoneum downward, freeing the hernia sac from the abdominal wall. The defect size is measured using the metallic portion of the instrument, just under five centimeters. Pneumoperitoneum is decreased to ten millimeters mercury, and the defect is closed with O-barb suture in a continuous running fashion.

A 13x15 cm macroporous polypropylene mesh is introduced through an 8 mm trocar and positioned flat against the abdominal wall. The mesh is secured with tacks placed at cardinal positions, and subsequently re-peritonealized with additional tacks.
